coredata.dc:title: Novel green CQDs/ZnO binary photocatalyst synthesis for efficient visible light irradiation of organic dye degradation for environmental remediation
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Shalini, S.
Sasikala, T.
Tharani, D.
Venkatesh, R.
Muthulingam, S.
coredata.dc:description: The advancement of effective and sustainable water treatment depends on the creation of a highly active and controllable photocatalyst. A suitable site for increasing the activity of such a photocatalyst is the heterojunction. Organic dyes have become a major source of concern in recent years because they are so common in wastewater. Heavy metals have a high permanence in wastewater and may be carcinogenic. Low-waste, high-performance technologies are needed to remove organic dyes and heavy metals from wastewater. To determine their structural, photochemical, and optical properties, Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) (made from Tamarindus indica shell waste), ZnO (from Ficus Benghalensis), and CQDs/ZnO photocatalysts were synthesised using green route techniques, followed by calcination. Malachite Green (MG) and Methylene Blue (MB) are effectively photo-degraded by the synthesised CQDs, ZnO, and Green CQDs/ZnO Binary Photocatalyst under the irradiation of naturally occurring solar light. The maximum photocatalytic activity for MG and MB within 60 min was shown by the Green CQDs/ZnO Binary Photocatalyst, with 100 % and 100 %, respectively. The reduced rate of electron-hole recombination causes effective photocatalytic activity. According to experiments with free radical entrapment, O2 and h+ play an important role in photodegradation. The study thus presents a fresh approach to the synthesis of highly effective binary photocatalysts for their application in environmental remediation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: ZnO NPs
CQDS/ZNO
Tamarindus Indica
Ficus Benghalensis
Environmental degradation
Photocatalytic Activity

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) and CQDs/ZnO
Proposed mechanism of photo catalytic activity of mg and mb dye assisted by pure zno and marigold flower-like CQDs/ZnO nanomaterials under solar light irradiation

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation of ZnO Nanoparticles via Green Synthesis Method.
XRD spectrum of ZnO and CQDs/ZnO nanocomposite.
SEM images of ZnO nanomaterials prepared from (Ficus Benghalensis Aerial Root), using green route techniques.
SEM images of marigold Flower –like CQDs/ZnO nanomaterials.
TEM images of marigold flower-like CQDs/ZnO.
EDX Spectra of (a) ZnO and (b) flower-like CQDs/ZnO.
Raman Spectra of (a) ZnO and (b) marigold flower-like CQDs/ZnO.
FT- IR spectrum of ZnO and marigold flower-like CQDs/ZnO.
UV–Visible Spectrum of (a) ZnO and (b) marigold flower-like CQDs/ZnO.
Band gap structure of ZnO and marigold flower-like CQDs/ZnO.
(1): XPS spectra of core level Zn 2p and O 1 s of CQD/ZnO composites.
The photocatalytic degradation behavior of Malachite Green dye with ZnO and marigold flower-like CQDs/ZnO nanomaterials.
The photocatalytic degradation behavior of Methylene Blue dye with ZnO and flower-like CQDs/ZnO nanomaterials.
Composition of elements present in zno and marigold flower-like cqds/zno by edax analysis.

head.ce:title: Novel green CQDs/ZnO binary photocatalyst synthesis for efficient visible light irradiation of organic dye degradation for environmental remediation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Nehru Institute of Engineering and Technology, Coimbatore 636005, Tamilnadu, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Nehru Institute of Engineering and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Nehru Institute of Engineering and Technology, Coimbatore 636005, Tamilnadu, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Sri Ramakrishna College of Arts & Science, Coimbatore 641006, Tamilnadu, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Sri Ramakrishna College of Arts & Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Sri Ramakrishna College of Arts & Science, Coimbatore 641006, Tamilnadu, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Sona College of Technology, Salem 636005, Tamilnadu, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Sona College of Technology, Salem 636005, Tamilnadu, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Annapoorna Engineering College, Salem 636308, Tamilnadu, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Annapoorna Engineering College, Salem 636308, Tamilnadu, India

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Chemistry, Annapoorana Engineering Colege, Salem, Taminadu, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The advancement of effective and sustainable water treatment depends on the creation of a highly active and controllable photocatalyst. A suitable site for increasing the activity of such a photocatalyst is the heterojunction. Organic dyes have become a major source of concern in recent years because they are so common in wastewater. Heavy metals have a high permanence in wastewater and may be carcinogenic. Low-waste, high-performance technologies are needed to remove organic dyes and heavy metals from wastewater. To determine their structural, photochemical, and optical properties, Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) (made from Tamarindus indica shell waste), ZnO (from Ficus Benghalensis), and CQDs/ZnO photocatalysts were synthesised using green route techniques, followed by calcination. Malachite Green (MG) and Methylene Blue (MB) are effectively photo-degraded by the synthesised CQDs, ZnO, and Green CQDs/ZnO Binary Photocatalyst under the irradiation of naturally occurring solar light. The maximum photocatalytic activity for MG and MB within 60 min was shown by the Green CQDs/ZnO Binary Photocatalyst, with 100 % and 100 %, respectively. The reduced rate of electron-hole recombination causes effective photocatalytic activity. According to experiments with free radical entrapment, O2 and h+ play an important role in photodegradation. The study thus presents a fresh approach to the synthesis of highly effective binary photocatalysts for their application in environmental remediation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The pollution of water resources by different organic contaminants has been one of the key problems in recent decades. The primary dangerous organic pollutants in nature with intricate structures are pesticides, insecticides, herbicides, medicines, aromatic hydrocarbons, and dyes. The environment and human health are at risk from the most hazardous chemicals, particularly dyes. Effective treatment methods are needed to convert organic contaminants into simple, non-hazardous compounds since they are poisonous and carcinogenic. [1–4]. Metal-oxide-based heterogeneous photocatalysis is one of the most efficient ways to eliminate organic contaminants in order to lessen their impact on water resources. Due to their captivating qualities, TiO2 and ZnO are two significant semiconductor metal oxides that are frequently used. A range of physical, chemical, and biological techniques are also used to remediate dyeing waste water, including adsorption [5], solid membrane filtration, and biological degradation [6]. Recent years have seen an increase in the use of Advanced Oxidation Processes (AOPs), which remediate waste water contaminants by using the hydroxyl radical as an oxidant agent. The most sophisticated AOP technology is the photocatalytic oxidation of contaminants utilising semiconducting metal oxides [7]. New adsorbents are also employed to remove dyes from simulated waste water, including biomass, nanomaterial composites [8–10], clays/zeolites composites [11–15], metal–organic frameworks [16], Chitosan composites [17], sawdust composites [18], bark, metal hydroxide sludge, fly ash, and red mud. [19,20]. The printing, textile, and paper industries frequently utilize the synthetic anionic azo dyes Malachite Green (MG) and Methylene Blue (MB). The ecology is at risk because of the incorrect disposal of industrial waste water, including MG and MB dye, which lowers the photosynthetic activity of aquatic plants. [21]. Due to rising environmental concerns and societal demands, clay-based materials have drawn the attention of scientists and industries as the most promising photocatalysts. Due to inherent benefits like the abundance of possible compositions and metal-anion exchange properties, low cost, and non-toxicity, CQDs and their derivative nanomaterials have enabled enormous advancements in effective pollution treatment and have also piqued the interest of multidisciplinary researchers. In addition, it possesses special qualities like strong chemical stability, pH-dependent solubility, and good biocompatibility. [22,23]. They can be used as promising drug delivery systems, PO4 adsorbents, new vitamin C drug delivery systems, anticancer reagents, photocatalysts, CO2 traps, nuclear waste management agents, and antacid agents due to the unique inherited properties of these materials, such as non-toxicity, replaceable intercalated anions, and chemical multifunctionality. The simple design of highly active, stable, low-cost, and promising catalysts with multiple interfaces has the potential to significantly increase catalytic activity. [29–46]. The aim of the current study is to: (i) prepare CQDs (Tamarindus indica shell waste), ZnO (Ficus Benghalensis aerial root extracts), and CQDs/ZnO nanocomposite; (ii) to compare the material characterization of CQDs, ZnO, and CQDs/ZnO; (iii) study the percentage removal of organic pollutants for CQDs/ZnO towards Methylene Blue (MB) and Malachite Green (MG) dye as a model organic pollutant in aqueous solutions under solar light irradiation; and (iv) understand the mechanism of action of CQDs/ZnO towards dye degradation. The novelty of this research lies in its green synthesis approach (If traditional methods of synthesis involve harmful chemicals or processes, a green approach would utilise safer alternatives, reducing environmental impact but this green synthesis process likely involves eco-friendly or sustainable methods), the use of novel Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs are a relatively new class of carbon nanomaterials that exhibit unique optical and electronic properties, the specific properties or characteristics of these CQDs, such as their size, morphology, surface chemistry, or optical properties), the development of a binary photocatalyst system (This combination of binary photocatalyst could lie in the specific method of combining these materials, optimising their interaction for maximum efficiency in organic dye degradation under visible light irradiation), its efficiency under visible light irradiation (Traditional photocatalysts like titanium dioxide (TiO2) are primarily active under UV light, which represents only a small portion of solar radiation. The ability of this novel binary photocatalyst to utilise visible light for organic dye degradation indicates a significant advancement. This expands the range of applications, especially in outdoor environments where UV light may be limited), and its application in organic dye degradation (Developing efficient photocatalysts for their degradation is crucial for sustainable water management) for environmental remediation (The ultimate goal of this research is environmental remediation—addressing pollution and improving the quality of water resources. This research lies in the potential of this binary photocatalyst system to offer a more effective and sustainable solution compared to existing methods).

Zinc acetate dihydrate (Zn(CH3COO)2·2H2O), ammonium hydroxide (NH4OH), and citric acid were purchased in analytical grade from Merck. Methylene Blue (MB) and Malachite Green (MG) dyes were purchased from Loba Chem (P) Ltd.

Fresh Ficus Benghalensis aerial roots were collected and washed with distilled water. The plant extract was prepared by boiling 25 g of aerial root in 100 mL of ethanol at 80 °C for 2 h. The plant extract is filtered after being stirred in a magnetic stirrer until the coloration shifts from light brown to dark brown. The plant extract is stored in a cold environment to avoid decomposition. ZnO nanoparticles are prepared by combining 25 mL of a stored extract with 25 mL (1 mM) of zinc acetate dihydrate Zn (CH3COO)2·2H2O The prepared solution is stirred well using a stirrer for 24 h at 50 °C, and the pH of 12.0 is maintained by adding 0.5 M ammonium hydroxide (NH4OH). Finally, the white precipitate is obtained. The collected sample is washed several times with water and ethanol to remove impurities using a centrifuge. Finally, the precipitate is dried in a hot air oven at 100 °C and calcined at 400 °C for 3 h in a muffle furnace.

The Tamarindus indica shell waste was dried and crushed into powders. The shell waste was kept in a furnace for 400 °C to carbonise the compound. The ash was collected and made into a fine powder by using a mortar and pestle.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) and CQDs/ZnO
ce:section.ce:para: Initially, 2 g of yielded carbon (Tamarindus indica shell waste) and 2 ml of citric acid were added. The reaction mixture was then well mixed with 30 ml of double-distilled water, and 5 ml of ammonium hydroxide was added to the solution to maintain pH 7. The scattered solution was put into a 50-ml beaker. The dispersed solution was transferred to a 50-ml beaker and kept in a domestic microwave oven for 30 min. After 30 min, the oven was allowed to cool naturally to room temperature. The reaction mixture was centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 1 h in ambient air after being ultrasonically treated for 50 min. Separate samples of the precipitate and supernatant were taken for further evaluation. For the CQD/ZnO photocatalyst, the necessary volume (10 lL) of CQD solution was combined with 50 mg of green synthesised ZnO nanocrystals in 25 mL of ethanol, and the mixture was agitated for 30 min at room temperature, filtered, repeatedly rinsed with ethanol, and dried.

Malachite green dye (MG dye) and Methylene blue dye (MB dye) were used as model pollutants for the photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants, and the photocatalyst was dissolved in the dye solution before the mixture was exposed to solar radiation to compare the catalytic effectiveness of ZnO and CQDs/ZnO. The dye and composite solution are held in the dark prior to radiation for 10 min to achieve adsorption–desorption equilibrium. From there, 0.005 g of synthesised nanocomposites was mixed with 50 ml of 1 mM MG dye in a 100 mL beaker. Following that, the mixture was exposed to sunlight for 15 min. Following the radiation, 5 ml of the sample was taken, centrifuged, and the filterate's absorbance was assessed. Using MB dye, similar studies were conducted. The change in concentration of the solution was tracked by a UV-spectrophotometer because MG dye had a maximum value at 612 nm. The formula was used to calculate the percentage of photocatalytic dye degradation.
P e r c e n t a g e
d y e
r e m o v a l
=
C 0
-
C t
C 0
X
100
where C0 (mg/L) − the initial concentrations of dyeCt (mg/L) − the concentrations of dye at time‘t’

The as-synthesised nanocomposite was characterised by a high-resolution powder Bruker D8 advance X-ray diffractometer with Cu Kα (1.5406 Å) radiation. The UV–Vis spectrum of green synthesised ZnO and CQDs/ZnO nanocomposites was recorded in the range of 190–1100 nm. (Cary 8454; Agilent, Singapore) with a resolution of 0.1 nm. The morphology and topography of the photocatalyst were investigated using FE-SEM, and elemental analysis was performed using EDX images from a SAIF-JEOL Model JSM-6390LV microscope taken at an accelerated voltage of 20 kV. Fourier Transformations Infrared spectra were recorded using a FT-IR spectrometer (Shimadzu model 8400S), and the samples were analysed in the region 4000–500 cm1 using KBr pellets. The Raman spectroscopy studies were performed using Brucker RFS 27. (Fig. 1 ).

X-ray diffraction (XRD) research revealed the crystal structure and phase composition of ZnO and CQD/ZnO composites. The XRD patterns of ZnO and CQD/ZnO composites are shown in Fig. 2 . The diffraction peaks could be precisely indexed to the hexagonal phase of ZnO, according to the XRD spectra, which also demonstrated that the ZnO was well crystallised (JCPDS no. 79–0206). Primary diffraction peaks were observed at 2θ (degree) values of 31.7°, 33.4°, 36.2°, 45.2°, 56.8°, and 63.1°, signifying the ZnO crystal planes (100), (002), (101), (102), (110), and (103). The relative intensities of these peaks are characteristic of ZnO’s hexagonal wurtzite structure. The expanded XRD patterns between 35 and 40° clearly show a minor shift in the graphitic (002) diffraction peak, which is responsible for a wider diffraction peak that developed near 26° for the CQD/ZnO composite. This suggested that the CQDs had been effectively coated on the surface of ZnO.

The SEM images of ZnO and CQDs/ZnO nanocomposite, which were produced by using green synthesis procedures, are shown in Figs. 3 and 4
. The unique morphological forms of ZnO and CQDs/ZnO nanocomposite that emerged during crystal formation are readily visible in the SEM images. It was very difficult to identify specific ZnO nanomaterial morphologies in the absence of CQDs, and as shown in Fig. 3, the majority of ZnO materials were found as nanosheets or nanoflakes. The growth of ZnO nanoparticles, however, became more regularised in the presence of CQDs, and a regular and uniform size distribution of CQDs and ZnO nanomaterials was formed, as shown in Figs. 4 and 5. The CQDs/ZnO nanomaterials strongly resemble a marigold flower (Tagestes, Daisy Family), according to their botanical name. By using a transmission electron microscope (TEM), the appearance of marigold flower-like CQDs/ZnO nanomaterials was further validated. Fig. 5 displays TEM pictures of the green synthesis-produced marigold flower-like CQDs/ZnO nanomaterials. It supports the creation of individually well-isolated ZnO nanoparticles that resemble marigold flowers (Fig. 5).

Furthermore, the high-resolution TEM image shows that no crystalline defects, such as stacking or dislocation faults were formed during the formation of the marigold-flower-like CQD/ZnO nanomaterials. According to Fig. 5, the distance between the two ZnO crystal planes (0, 0, 2) and the observed lattice space between two neighboring lattice planes noted from the HRTEM pictures is 51 nm. The length and diameter of marigold flower-like CQDs/ZnO nanostructures were measured using a high-magnification TEM image.

Using a measurement device found in the TEM instrument, the average diameter of each CQD/ZnO nanomaterial that resembled a marigold flower was determined. An individual CQDs/ZnO marigold flower, which is made up of multiple tiny wings that sprout from the flower's centre, has an average diameter of 200 nm. Thus, as shown in Fig. 5, the average diameter of the individual wings was determined to be between 40 and 55 nm. As a result, we think the synthetic CQD/ZnO marigold flower is in the nanometer range. The ZnO marigold nano-flowers' selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns were also captured; the presence of numerous brilliant spots on the SAED pattern proves that the marigold flower-like CQDs/ZnO nanomaterials are polycrystalline in origin. In order to ensure the role of CQDs in the growth of marigold flower-like CQDs/ZnO architectures, the SEM images were recorded. Surprisingly, the SEM images reveal an identical structural morphology rather than distinctly diverse morphologies. However, the length, smoothness, and diameter of each wing are varying with respect to different CQDs/ZnO architectures prepared from green synthesis methods, as shown in Fig. 5. Therefore, based on this result, we believe that marigold flower-like CQDs/ZnO architectures can be readily grown by introducing CQDs during growth.

The Energy Dispersive X-ray (EDX) analysis was conducted concurrently with morphological scanning of ZnO and marigold flower-like Carbon Quantum Dots/Zinc Oxide (CQDs/ZnO) composites, as depicted in Fig. 6 . The mass percentages of the detected elements, determined through EDX analysis, are presented in Table 1 .

The identification of a Zn peak with a mass percentage of 4.64 % in the marigold flower-like CQDs/ZnO indicates the presence of ZnO within the composite. This finding suggests a homogeneous distribution of the elements Zn, O, and C within the structure. The data supports the formation of an intercalated compound between the CQDs and ZnO, highlighting the successful integration and dispersion of these components within the composite framework.

Raman spectroscopy was utilized to assess the phase and purity of both ZnO and marigold flower-like CQDs/ZnO composites. Fig. 7 displays the Raman spectra, where both ZnO and the marigold flower-like CQDs/ZnO exhibit two prominent peaks: one around 250 cm-1 corresponding to the Metal-O band and another around 1320 cm-1 associated with carbon bands. These peaks result from the asymmetric stretching vibrations of the Metal-O bands.

The presence of these peaks confirms that the CQDs are uniformly integrated onto the ZnO structure. Furthermore, the Raman spectra indicate that the ZnO retains stable microstructural characteristics, achieved through the lattice atomic-confined in situ reduction strategy. This method ensures the uniform growth and stable incorporation of CQDs on the ZnO catalyst.

The FTIR spectra of ZnO and marigold flower-like CQDs/ZnO, depicted in Fig. 8 , reveal several characteristic peaks indicative of their chemical structures. The broad peak at approximately 3500 cm-1 is attributed to the presence of OH groups and water molecules. The peak at 1620 cm-1 corresponds to the bending vibration of the OH group in water molecules, while the peak at 1380 cm-1 is associated with the bending vibration of the CO group. In the region from 400 to 900 cm-1, the peaks are assigned to the lattice vibration modes of metal oxide and metal-hydroxide groups. Specifically, the band around 670 cm-1 is designated for the lattice vibration mode of ZnOH, and the band near 530 cm-1 corresponds to ZnO vibrations. Additionally, lattice vibration bands of C-OH and Zn-O are observed at 748 cm-1 and 586 cm-1, respectively. The FTIR spectra of ZnO and CQDs/ZnO also display some additional IR bands, which confirm the encapsulation of ZnO into CQDs and the formation of intercalated compounds. These additional bands indicate successful integration and interaction between ZnO and CQDs within the composite material.

The UV–visible spectrum of green synthesised ZnO and marigold flower-like CQDs/ZnO nanoparticles was recorded in the range of 200–800 nm. The recorded spectrum is shown in Fig. 9 (a and b). The maximum absorbance band was noticed in the spectrum of ZnO (269 nm and 378 nm) and marigold flower-like CQDs/ZnO (300 nm and 390 nm). It is observed that the CQDs are successfully decorated on the ZnO surface. ZnO was found to have a band gap of 3.21 eV, but marigold flower-like CQD/ZnO showed a somewhat lower value of 3.10 eV (Fig. 10 ). A longer wavelength and lower energy caused the absorption edge to move. It has been observed that transition-metal (TM) doping of ZnO nanoparticles doped with Co, Ni, and Cr, as well as other TM-doped semiconductor nanoparticle systems [56–61], causes a similar change in the absorption edge and band gap energy. As the dopant concentration increased, a decrease in the diffuse reflectance was also seen, in addition to a fall in the band gap energy. It was determined that the primary cause of the redshift in the band gap energy observed after dopant was incorporated into the ZnO lattice was the sp-d exchange interactions between the band electrons and the localised d electrons of the dopant ions that were replacing the Zn ions [62]. The conduction band and the valence band edges are thought to be positively and negatively corrected by the s-d and p-d exchange interactions, which narrow the band gap [62,63], suggesting that the CQD/ZnO composite had a wider absorption range. Therefore, even in the visible range, it might be advised to use it as an efficient photocatalyst. Fig. 11 (1) (a)–(d) shows the high-resolution XPS spectra of core level Zn 2p and O 1 s of CQD/ZnO composites. The full survey spectrum in demonstrates that the occurrence of main elements such as Zn, O and C in the CQD/ZnO composites. The binding energy values related to the Zn 2p 3/2 and Zn 2p1/2 were observed at 1017 eV and 1041 eV respectively. Also, the deconvolution of O 1 s XPS spectrum exhibits a main peak with a binding energy of 527.2 eV representing to the OZnO bond and the other higher binding energy values at 528.3 eV and 529.6 eV are corresponding to the oxygen defects within the ZnO materials.

For the photocatalytic experiments, two different organic dyes, Malachite Green (MG) and Methylene Blue (MB), were chosen to carry out dye degradation in the presence of ZnO and marigold flower-like CQDs/ZnO nanomaterials under solar light exposure. Before irradiation, the dye and composite solution are kept in the dark for 15 min to obtain adsorption–desorption equilibrium. From there, 50 ml of the required concentration of MG dye was taken into a 100-mL beaker, and the required amount of synthesised ZnO and marigold flower-like CQDs/ZnO nanomaterials were added to it. Then the mixture was irradiated under naturally available daylight sources for 30 min. After a given daylight exposure time, about 5 mL of the mixture was withdrawn, and the catalysts were separated from the suspension prior to measuring the photodegradation. Dye degradation was measured by considering the chances in the absorption band of MG dye at 612 nm with respect to light exposure time. Photocatalytic measurements were performed under naturally available daylight sources. The daylight intensity (35–105 ± 5000 lx) was measured by a digital luxmeter between May and June 2022 (10 a.m. and 2p.m.). All the experiments were carried out three times. The error was below 5 %, and average values were taken. The percentage of photocatalytic dye degradation was calculated by using the formula.
P e r c e n t a g e
d y e
r e m o v a l =
C 0
-
C t
C 0
X 100
where C0 (mg/L) − the initial concentrations of MG dyeCt (mg/L) − the concentrations of MG dye at time‘t’

Fig. 13 shows the photocatalytic activity of Methylene Blue dye solution with respect to different ZnO and flower-like CQDs/ZnO nanomaterials grown from CQDs (Tamarindus indica shell waste), ZnO (Ficus Benghalensis aerial root), and CQDs/ZnO photocatalysts synthesised using green route techniques. It was noted that a strong characteristic UV absorption peak existed at 654 nm for the pure MB dye. However, the intensity of the UV absorption peak was largely suppressed by the photocatalytic activity of ZnO and marigold flower-like CQDs/ZnO nanomaterials after 60 min of UV irradiation. The photograph was taken right after the photocatalytic reaction after 60 min, and the larger difference in the colour of the dye solution before and after photocatalytic treatment can be seen, which proves that ZnO and flower-like CQDs/ZnO nanomaterials are efficient photocatalysts for organic dyes.

The dye degradation percentage based on the intensity of pure MB dye at 654 nm, pure ZnO, and marigold flower-like CQDs/ZnO nanomaterials before and after photocatalytic treatment was measured to be 6.8 %, 78.9 %, and 100 %, respectively. These results indicate that the higher surface-to-volume ratio of marigold flower-like CQDs/ZnO nanomaterials helps to accommodate a higher degree of dye molecular adsorption on their surface and leads to degradation upon naturally available daylight excitation. Marigold flower-like CQDs/ZnO nanomaterials behaved as excellent photocatalysts, and a complete decoloration of organic dyes occurred even after 60 min of solar light irradiation.

The % photodegradation of the CQD/ZnO composite was compared with previously published results for other metal oxide photocatalysts adorned with CQDs in order to verify the photocatalytic performance of the CQD/ZnO composites. For example, it has been reported that the addition of H2O2 to a CQD/a-Fe2O3 photocatalyst under visible light will degrade methylene blue (MB) dye in 90 min (Yu et al. 2012). It has been observed that after two hours of UV light irradiation, a CQD/ZnO heterostructure degrades 80 % of the RhB dye (Li et al. 2013). Similar to this, Zhao et al. (2010) found that a C/TiO2 photocatalyst required at least 4 h of visible light irradiation in order to degrade roughly 80 % of the methyl orange dye.

When exposed to UV radiation, a ZnO/graphene photocatalyst demonstrated over 90 % degradation of MB dye in 40 min (Xu et al. 2011). Under visible light, a photocatalyst of TiO2/C-dots required at least 4 h to degrade MB dye; however, under near-IR light, with CQDs/Cu2O, only 90 % of the MB dye was degraded in 4 h (Li et al. 2011). Thus, we deduced that the combined impact of both the CQDs and the doping properties was the main source of the CQD/ZnO composite's enhanced photocatalytic performance (Figs. 12 and 13).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Proposed mechanism of photo catalytic activity of mg and mb dye assisted by pure zno and marigold flower-like CQDs/ZnO nanomaterials under solar light irradiation
ce:section.ce:para:
The proposed mechanism for the decoloration of MG Dye (Fig. 12) is shown below:

2

CQDs/ZnO, which resemble marigold flowers, behave as doped semiconductors and produce electron-hole pairs when exposed to sunlight irradiation. The reduction process results in the formation of superoxide radicals (O2 •-) from the scavenging of the photo-generated electrons in the conduction band by the adsorbed oxygen molecules on the catalyst surface. The adsorbed water molecule is drawn to the hole in the valence band, forming reactive hydroxyl species (OH•-), which is the oxidation process. [64–69] Highly active species such as superoxide and hydroxyl radicals decolorize the dye molecules in the following phases. Under solar light irradiation, the dye molecule degrades through a free radical chain reaction that is started by superoxide and hydroxyl radicals. It was discovered that CQDs/ZnO had layered structures, a large surface area, and excellent sorption capability. Due to these characteristics, the rate of photodecolorization rises when CQDs or ZnO are used as photocatalysts. It absorbs more dye molecules on the outer surface as well as the intergallery surface. [48–55]. Fig. 12 depicts the similar method used in the MB dye with marigold flowers like CQDs or ZnO.

We report a green synthesised method to synthesise ZnO and marigold flower-like CQDs/ZnO nanomaterials. The CQDS molecules immobilise the intermediate zinc ammonium complex ions [Zn (NH3)4]2+, which act as ZnO nucleation sites. The high solubility of the intermediate zinc ammonium [Zn (NH3)4]2 + complex ion leads to the supply of zinc ions for further growth to reach distinct CQDS/ZnO nanomaterials. These marigold flower-like CQDs/ZnO nanomaterials could be used as an efficient photocatalyst for decomposing organic dye molecules upon solar light irradiation. Two different organic dyes were used to prove the photocatalytic activity of ZnO and marigold flower-like CQDs/ZnO nanomaterials under solar light irradiation. ZnO materials synthesised using the green route technique proved capable of degrading dye molecules under solar light irradiation for a specific period of time. A maximum of 1 h was adequate to degrade 100 % of malachite green under solar irradiation in the presence of marigold flower-like CQDs and ZnO nanomaterials. Therefore, these types of materials produced using green synthesised growth can be used as an efficient photocatalyst for dye degradation.

S. Shalini: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Project administration. T. Sasikala: Data curation, Formal analysis, Supervision, Validation, Writing – review & editing. D. Tharani: Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Validation, Visualization. R. Venkatesh: Data curation, Formal analysis. S. Muthulingam: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are thankful to Dr.V.Anbuchezian Principal, Annapoorna Engineering College, Salem, Tamilnadu, India for all the necessary facilitates in the college.

ce:section.ce:para: Not applicable.
Not applicable.
Yes. All authors agreed to participate in this research.
yes. All authors have approved the last version of the manuscript for its submission.

Mater. Res. Bull.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Haodong Tang, Wujian Zhang, Yue Meng, Bo Xie, Zheming Ni, Shengjie Xia, Investigation onto the performance and mechanism of visible light photodegradation of methyl orange catalyzed by M/CeO2 (M=Pt, Ag, Au), Materials Research Bulletin, Volume 144, 2021, 111497, ISSN 0025-5408, DOI: 10.1016/j.materresbull.2021.111497

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Visible light active TiO2/CS/Fe3O4 for nitrophenol degradation: Studying impact of TiO2, CS and Fe3O4 loading on the optical and photocatalytic performance of nanocomposite
Mater. Sci. Semicond. Process.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Saba Afzal, Nurhidayatullaili Muhd Julkapli, Lee Kian Mun, Visible light active TiO2/CS/Fe3O4 for nitrophenol degradation: Studying impact of TiO2, CS and Fe3O4 loading on the optical and photocatalytic performance of nanocomposite, Materials Science in Semiconductor Processing, Volume 131, 2021, 105891, ISSN 1369-8001, DOI: 10.1016/j.mssp.2021.105891

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: G. Mishra, B. Dash, S. Pandey. (2018) “Layered double hydroxides: A brief review from fundamentals to application as evolving biomaterials,” Applied Clay Science. 153. Elsevier, pp. 172–186. doi: 10.1016/j.clay.2017.12.021.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Layered double hydroxides of CaAl: a promising drug delivery system for increased dissolution rate and thermal stability of praziquantel
Appl. Clay Sci.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. R. R. Timóteo et al., “Layered double hydroxides of CaAl: A promising drug delivery system for increased dissolution rate and thermal stability of praziquantel,” Appl. Clay Sci., vol. 180, no. March, p. 105197, 2019, doi: 10.1016/j.clay.2019.105197.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: P. Gu et al. (2018) “Recent advances in layered double hydroxide-based nanomaterials for the removal of radionuclides from aqueous solution,” Environmental Pollution, vol. 240. Elsevier Ltd, pp. 493–505. doi: 10.1016/j.envpol.2018.04.136.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Preparation and characterization of magnetic Fe3O4@sulfonated β-cyclodextrin intercalated layered double hydroxides for methylene blue removal
Desalin. Water Treat.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Hu, X. Wu, F. Jiao, W. Yang, and Y. Zhou, “Preparation and characterization of magnetic Fe3O4@sulfonated β-cyclodextrin intercalated layered double hydroxides for methylene blue removal,” Desalination and Water Treatment, vol. 57, no. 53. pp. 25830–25841, 2016, doi: 10.1080/19443994.2016.1155173.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Zn-Al layered double hydroxides as efficient photocatalysts for NOx abatement
Chem. Eng. J.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Rodriguez-Rivas, A. Pastor, C. Barriga, M. Cruz-Yusta, L. Sánchez, and I. Pavlovic, “Zn-Al layered double hydroxides as efficient photocatalysts for NOx abatement,” Chem. Eng. J., vol. 346, no. April, pp. 151–158, 2018, doi: 10.1016/j.cej.2018.04.022.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Green synthesis for novel sorbent of sand coated with (Ca/Al)-layered double hydroxide for the removal of toxic dye from aqueous environment
J. Environ. Chem. Eng.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. A. H. Faisal, A. H. Shihab, M. Naushad, T. Ahamad, G. Sharma, and K. M. Al-Sheetan, “Green synthesis for novel sorbent of sand coated with (Ca/Al)-layered double hydroxide for the removal of toxic dye from aqueous environment,” J. Environ. Chem. Eng., vol. 9, no. 4, p. 105342, 2021, doi: 10.1016/j.jece.2021.105342.
